class Item(models.Model): name = models.CharField(max_length=100) skills = models.ManyToManyField('Skill', blank=True)
class Skill(models.Model): name = models.CharField(max_length=100) Everything worked flawlessly in my local development environment. But in staging and UAT(Pre-Production), attempting to assign skills to or instances would:Return success responses from the API.Show no errors in the Django admin panel.But fail to actually create the relationships.My first instinct was to blame the frontend. Maybe there was an issue with payload encryption or data serialization? After thorough investigation, the frontend was innocent.
Network and Concurrency Theories Next, I suspected network issues—perhaps packet loss was causing incomplete requests. I also considered race conditions: maybe the main model transaction wasn't committed before Django tried to create the M2M relationships. Both theories proved incorrect.The breakthrough came when I remembered that a few days prior, we had populated staging and UAT databases using a backup restore. During subsequent operations, we started encountering this error:django.db.utils.IntegrityError: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "django_migrations_pkey" DETAIL: Key (id)=(3) already exists. This was the smoking gun that led me to the real issue: PostgreSQL sequence desynchronization.
Understanding PostgreSQL Sequences When you restore data into PostgreSQL from a backup, the records themselves are inserted correctly, but the sequences (which control the next value for auto-incrementing primary keys) aren’t automatically updated (Sadly!). This causes a subtle but potentially destructive mismatch:Your table could have rows with IDs up to 10000s.However, the sequence still assumes the next available ID is 1, as if the table were empty.Now, when Django tries to insert a new record, it attempts to use ID=1, which already exists, triggering a primary key conflict.I had resolved the main model sequence issues using this script:
The Actual Problem at hand : Implicit Through Tables The key realization that took me hours to uncover: Django’s implicit many-to-many through tables also have auto-incrementing ID fields with their own sequences. Plus, doesn’t return these implicit through tables.When you define a without specifying a model, Django automatically creates a through table with:An auto-incrementing fieldForeign keys to both related modelsIts own PostgreSQL sequenceThese implicit through table sequences were also desynchronized after the database restore!To fix the M2M relationships, I had to manually reset the sequences for all implicit through tables:You can discover the through table names programmatically:
A Comprehensive Django Management Command Here's a complete solution as a Django management command:: When creating backups, ensure sequences are included and properly handled: Always run sequence reset commands after database restores: Include M2M relationship tests in your deployment verification suite: Set up alerts for IntegrityError exceptions in productionSilent failures are the worst: M2M relationship failures can be completely silent, making them incredibly difficult to debug: Django's implicit through tables add hidden complexity that's easy to overlookEnvironment parity matters: Issues that don't reproduce locally can be the most challenging to solve: PostgreSQL sequence desynchronization is a common but often overlooked issue after database restoresThis debugging challenge sharpened my engineering skills and taught me valuable lessons about and . The issue stemmed from Django's many-to-many relationships, which use implicit tables with auto-incrementing IDs and sequences. If these sequences aren't properly managed, they can cause data conflicts and silent failures. This experience underscored the importance of understanding Django's database interactions and the need for careful sequence management in PostgreSQL to maintain relationship integrity.Crucially, whenever you restore data in a Django application, you must examine and reset sequences for through tables—not just your main model tables.

item.title: 🐍 Python Programming: From Basics to Control Flow
item.author: Abhishek Jaiswal
item.content:encoded:
Learning Python is like learning to ride a bike 🚴‍♂️ — at first, balancing feels tricky, but once you get it, you’ll never forget. In this blog, we’ll go step by step through and , covering everything from installation to loops, with clear explanations and examples you can try yourself.Python is one of the most popular programming languages today, and for good reason. Created by in 1991, inspired by ABC language. Not the snake 🐍, but after the comedy group !Easy to read and write (feels like English).Cross-platform (Windows, Mac, Linux).Large community & libraries (NumPy, Pandas, TensorFlow, Django, Flask).Versatile (AI, Web Dev, Automation, Data Science, Scripting).👉 Python vs Other Languages:Compared to Java/C++: Python is slower but far simpler and more productive.Compared to JavaScript: Python isn’t mainly for frontend, but dominates in data science and backend.Compared to R: Python is general-purpose, while R is specialized in statistics.
🔹 Python Syntax & Execution Python files use the extension. You can run code in two ways: → Directly in the Python shell (great for quick tests). → Save code in file and run it via .✅ Unlike C, C++, or Java:No semicolons required (unless multiple statements in one line).No curly braces . Instead, (spaces/tabs) defines code blocks.Variables are like containers storing data. In Python, you don’t declare types explicitly.👉 : You can reassign a variable to another type:, , , → collections: Reserved words like , , , , , . (You can’t use them as variable names.): Names you give to variables, functions, classes.Must start with a letter/underscore.Case-sensitive: ≠ . Use function. Use function. Always returns a string. Operators are symbols to perform operations on values.Now let’s move beyond basic syntax and start making decisions in our programs.Conditions let your program decide .👉 Adding more conditions with :Loops help us without rewriting code. loop → Iterates over a sequence: 👉 Looping through a list: loop → Runs until condition is false:
🔹 Loop Control Statements → exit loop immediately. → skip current iteration, move to next. → do nothing (placeholder).
🔹 Iterators & Generators (Intro) → An object you can loop through (e.g., list). → A function that yields values one at a time (saves memory).
🔹 List Comprehensions & Generator Expressions Python offers a to create lists and generators.This is the foundation of everything in Python. In the next stages, we will dive into functions, data structures, OOP, file handling, and advanced modules.🚀 Practice small coding exercises daily — build a calculator, a number guessing game, or automate a simple task. That’s how Python will truly “click” for you.

That’s why I built ContentAIPro, an AI-powered tool that helps developers, writers, and creators generate content faster and smarter.AI Content Generation: Create blog posts, articles, and social media captions in seconds.Customizable Prompts: Guide the AI to produce content in your style or tone.Multi-Platform Support: Generate content for websites, LinkedIn, Twitter, and more.SEO Friendly: Suggestions to optimize your content for better search visibility.Easy Integration: Works with APIs for automation in your workflow.AI: OpenAI GPT / Custom ML modelsFrontend: React + Tailwind CSSContentAIPro is designed to save time, boost creativity, and improve productivity for anyone who creates content regularly.Check it out and let me know your thoughts! item.title: Decoy-Hunter: Bypassing "All Ports Open" Deception in Offensive Security
item.author: KL3FT3Z
item.content:encoded:
Defensive deception is powerful — but not invisible. This tool helps red teams and penetration testers cut through the noise of fake services and find real attack surfaces.
🔥 The Problem: "All Ports Are Open" Deception In modern defensive architectures, it's becoming common to by making every TCP port appear open. This is often achieved with simple rules:iptables nat PREROUTING eth0 tcp conntrack NEW REDIRECT 1234 1nc 1234Or more advanced tools like , which return on every port.The goal? Waste the attacker’s time, trigger false positives in scanners, and hide real services among thousands of decoys.But here’s the catch: most deception implementations are flawed. returns different banners on each scan → easily detectable.Static banner emulators (e.g., using responses) .Fake services often with the same static string — real services don’t work that way.This creates an opportunity for offensive counter-deception.
🕵️‍♂️ Introducing is a stealthy, protocol-aware scanner designed to:Detect fake services behind "all ports open" traps,Identify real, exploitable services hidden in the noise,Operate with to avoid detection by the defender.Unlike , which can be fooled by consistent fake banners, :Uses to send legitimate client requests,Validates , not just banners,Supports up to port 10,000 (or full range),Mimics human-like timing and request patterns.Instead of sending raw strings, :Sends with real ,Initiates on HTTPS ports,Uses , , , etc.,Leverages the official database for accuracy.A fake SSH service might return , but:It won’t complete a key exchange,It won’t respond correctly to malformed packets,It may reply with an SSH banner on — which is suspicious.Does the response match the ?Is the service consistent across multiple probes?Does it only respond to one probe type, ignoring others?
3. Traffic Obfuscation (Anti-Detection)To avoid being flagged as a scanner:Random delays between connections (),Limited concurrency (default: 15),No aggressive payload spraying,TLS used where expected (e.g., port 443),No repeated identical patterns.🔒 : Make traffic look like a curious user or misconfigured client — not a pentest tool.
2. Download wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nmap/nmap/master/nmap-service-probes
python3 decoy_hunter.py 192.168.1.10
python3 decoy_hunter.py target.com 5
python3 decoy_hunter.py 10.0.0.5 22,80,443,8080,1234 [REAL] 22/tcp open ssh (via passive/NULL) → SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.9p1 [FAKE] 8080/tcp open http (via GetRequest) → SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.9p1 ← 🚩 Mismatch! [REAL] 443/tcp open http (via GetRequest) → HTTP/1.1 200 OK Notice: → clear sign of deception.Full supportAccurate service detection using Nmap’s official databaseFast, scalable scanning without blockingCovers both transport layersRandomized timing, realistic requests, low concurrency (except )Pure Python, easy to run anywhereDefensive deception is a — but it shouldn’t create a false sense of security. As red teamers and ethical hackers, we must:Understand defensive tricks,Develop tools to ,Help organizations of their deception layers. is not just a scanner — it’s a counter-deception framework for the modern attack surface.Found a fake service that missed? Have an idea to improve obfuscation or probe coverage?→ → Issues and feature requests encouraged.This tool is for authorized penetration testing and research only. Never scan systems you don’t own or don’t have explicit permission to test.decoy-hunter/ ├── decoy_hunter.py # Main CLI tool ├── probes.py # Probe logic & obfuscation ├── service_probes_parser.py # Parses nmap-service-probes ├── nmap-service-probes # (Download separately) ├── README.md # This file └── requirements.txt # tqdm 🔍 Real security isn’t about hiding — it’s about resilience. to ensure your defenses are tested, not just decorated.

item.title: Discover the Mystical World of Tarot with Python
item.author: Ricardo Casais
item.content:encoded: Introduction: Why I Built This Have you ever been curious about tarot readings but felt overwhelmed by the decks and interpretations? I wanted to create a simple, interactive way to explore tarot using Python. This project was born out of a desire to combine coding with the mystical world of tarot cards—making the experience accessible for beginners, while also being fun for enthusiasts.My Python program simulates a tarot reading by shuffling a deck of tarot cards, drawing a few, and displaying their meanings in a clear, user-friendly format. It uses object-oriented programming to manage the deck and cards, making the code modular and easy to extend.A complete tarot deck with both Major and Minor Arcana.Randomized shuffling for authentic readings.Clean formatting of card meanings for readability.Easy-to-use functions for drawing single or multiple cards.This project has been a rewarding journey, merging logic and intuition in one interactive experience. It’s a great starting point for anyone wanting to explore tarot, or just enjoy a little mystical fun. Future updates might include adding card reversals, interactive GUI, or integrating with web applications—but for now, it’s a solid foundation to explore the magical world of tarot through code.Try it out yourself and see what the cards reveal!

item.title: 🚀 Day 26 of My Python Learning Journey
item.author: Aditi Sharma
item.content:encoded: Hypothesis Testing in Data Analysis 📊Today I explored the backbone of statistical decision-making → Hypothesis Testing.🔹 Key Steps: 1. State Hypotheses • Null (H₀): No effect/difference • Alternative (H₁): There is an effect/difference 2. Set Significance Level (α) → usually 0.05 3. Choose a Test → t-test, chi-square, ANOVA, etc. 4. Calculate Test Statistic & P-Value 5. Make a Decision • Else → Fail to reject H₀✅ Validates assumptions ✅ Minimizes bias in decisions ✅ Powers research, A/B testing & analytics⚡ Fun Fact: Hypothesis testing was formalized by Ronald Fisher & Jerzy Neyman in the early 1900s — and it’s still the gold standard in science!

item.title: The Real Python Podcast – Episode #267: Managing Feature Flags & Comparing Python Visualization Libraries

item.title: Deploying MiroThinker for AI-Powered Predictions in askpaul.ai
item.author: AskPaul
item.content:encoded:
As we develop askpaul.ai, a prediction market application requiring accurate AI-powered event outcome forecasting, we needed a reliable and high-performance model to power our prediction engine. After evaluating several options, we chose MiroMind's MiroThinker model for its exceptional predictive capabilities. This blog outlines our deployment process on a CentOS system with GPU acceleration.Our deployment environment consists of:CentOS 8.3 operating systemNVIDIA H20 GPU for accelerated computing
Prerequisites Installation
1. Python 3.12 Installation We started by installing Python 3.12, which provides the necessary runtime environment for our application:dnf gcc openssl-devel bzip2-devel libffi-devel wget https://www.python.org/ftp/python/3.12.0/Python-3.12.0.tgz xzf Python-3.12.0.tgz Python-3.12.0 ./configure make altinstall To leverage GPU acceleration, we installed the NVIDIA CUDA Compiler (nvcc):dnf config-manager https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/rhel8/x86_64/cuda-rhel8.repo dnf cuda-toolkit Note about CentOS 8.3 package management: CentOS 8 and later versions use as the default package manager, which is an enhanced version of . While commands still work as aliases to , we recommend using directly for better performance and dependency resolution.We installed the following Python packages to ensure proper functionality:pip3.12 sglang pybase64 pydantic orjson uvicorn uvloop fastapi torch psutil zmq packaging Pillow openai partial_json_parser huggingface_hub transformers sentencepiece sgl_kernel dill compressed_tensors einops msgspec python-multipart pynvml torchao xgrammar openai_harmony These packages provide essential functionality including:Web serving capabilities (uvicorn, fastapi)GPU-accelerated tensor operations (torch, torchao)Model management and inference (sglang, transformers, huggingface_hub)Data processing and serialization (orjson, msgspec, pybase64)With all prerequisites in place, we deployed the MiroThinker-32B-DPO-v0.2 model using sglang's server:python3.12 sglang.launch_server miromind-ai/MiroThinker-32B-DPO-v0.2 1 1 0.0.0.0 6666 qwen3_nonthinking.jinja miromind.log & This command starts the server in the background with , ensuring it continues running even after logout. The model is deployed with:Tensor parallelism (tp) set to 1Data parallelism (dp) set to 1These settings are appropriate for our single GPU setup.Deploying MiroThinker on our CentOS 8.3 system with an H20 GPU has significantly enhanced the prediction capabilities of askpaul.ai. The model's performance meets our expectations for accuracy and response time, making it an excellent fit for our prediction market application.The sglang framework provided a straightforward deployment path, and the MiroThinker model has proven to be reliable and efficient in our production environment. We're excited to continue leveraging this powerful combination as we expand the capabilities of askpaul.ai.

item.title: Building a Secure Gmail CLI: From Development to Production 🚀
item.author: Vishwaraja Pathi (Vishwa)
item.content:encoded:
How I built a comprehensive Gmail command-line interface with security-first design, Docker containerization, and CI/CD automation.As developers, we live in our terminals. We manage code, deploy applications, and debug issues all from the command line. But when it comes to email management, we're forced to switch to web browsers or desktop applications, breaking our workflow.What if we could manage Gmail directly from the terminal? What if we could read emails, send messages, manage drafts, and configure settings without leaving our development environment?
💡 The Solution: Gmail CLI I built - a comprehensive command-line interface for Gmail that brings the full power of Gmail's API to your terminal. It's not just another email client; it's a developer-focused tool designed to integrate seamlessly into your workflow. with rich formatting and filtering with full HTML/text support with attachments and rich formatting with Gmail's powerful search syntax for efficient email management - Create, read, update, and send drafts - View and manage conversation threads - Create custom labels and organize emails - Configure filters, forwarding, and vacation responders - Download and manage email attachments - No password storage, secure token-based authSecure Credential Storage - Credentials stored in user home directoryEnvironment Variable Configuration - Flexible credential path management - Prevents accidental credential commits
��️ Technical Implementation The CLI is built with Python and leverages several key technologies: - Full access to Gmail's capabilities - Secure, token-based authentication - Beautiful command-line interface - Rich text formatting and progress bars - Containerized deployment for consistency
🔒 Security: The Most Important Feature
The Problem with Credentials One of the biggest challenges in building CLI tools is handling credentials securely. Too many projects accidentally commit sensitive files to version control, exposing API keys and tokens.
1. Secure Credential Storage ~/.gmail-cli/credentials.json ~/.gmail-cli/token.json
2. Environment Variable Configuration
3. # Google API credentials and tokens token.json credentials.json *.json !package.json !tsconfig.json !*.config.json
# Additional security patterns *.pem *.key *.p12 *.pfx secrets/ .secrets/
4. ./secure-setup.sh
🐳 Docker: Simplified and Secure
Why We Removed Docker Compose Initially, I used Docker Compose for the project, but I realized it was overkill for a single image. Docker Compose is great for multi-service applications, but for a single CLI tool, it adds unnecessary complexity. docker run /data:/app/data ~/.gmail-cli/credentials.json:/app/credentials.json:ro ~/.gmail-cli/token.json:/app/token.json vishwa86/gmail-cli:latest list
./docker-run.sh list ./docker-run.sh send user@example.com ./docker-run.sh shell The project includes a comprehensive CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions: - Runs tests on Python 3.8-3.11, linting, and security scans - Multi-architecture builds on every push - Pushes to Docker Hub on main branch merges - Automatic tagging for releases gmail list
gmail <message-id>
gmail search gmail mark <message-id>
gmail drafts create gmail drafts list
gmail drafts send <draft-id>
gmail download <message-id> <attachment-id>
gmail label create gmail settings vacation The CLI uses the Rich library to provide: for better readability for long operations for structured data display for email content
Comprehensive Help System
gmail help-detailed
gmail send gmail drafts Always design with security in mind from the startUse environment variables for configurationImplement comprehensive .gitignore rulesCreate automated security setup scripts
2. Simplicity Over ComplexityDocker Compose isn't always necessarySimple Docker commands can be more maintainableFocus on the core functionality firstRich terminal output improves usabilityComprehensive help systems reduce learning curveConsistent command structure across all operationsCI/CD pipelines catch issues earlyAutomated security scanning prevents vulnerabilitiesMulti-architecture builds ensure compatibility with Gmail API enabled downloaded from Google Cloud Console (optional but recommended) git clone https://github.com/vishwaraja/gmail-cli.git gmail-cli ./docker-setup.sh ./secure-setup.sh ./docker-run.sh auth
gmail auth
gmail list The project is open source and welcomes contributions:: Help improve stability: Suggest new functionality: Submit pull requests: Improve guides and examplesGmail CLI represents more than just a command-line email client. It's a testament to the power of developer-focused tools that integrate seamlessly into existing workflows. By bringing Gmail to the terminal, we're not just changing how we manage emails—we're reimagining the developer experience.The journey from initial development to production-ready tool taught me valuable lessons about security, simplicity, and automation. The most important takeaway: security should be designed in from the start, not bolted on later.Whether you're a terminal enthusiast, a DevOps engineer, or simply someone who values efficiency, Gmail CLI offers a new way to interact with one of the most important communication tools in our digital lives.Ready to transform your email workflow? Give Gmail CLI a try and experience the power of terminal-based email management.What's your experience with command-line email tools? Have you tried Gmail CLI? Share your thoughts and experiences in the comments below!

item.title: Julien Tayon: Abusing yahi -a log based statistic tool à la awstats- to plot histograms/date series from CSV
From Idea to Deployment with FastAPI and Postgres
item.author: Pedro Kauati
item.content:encoded:
Hello! My name is Pedro Kauati👋In this post I'm going to describe my experience creating a webapp using FastAPI⚡: I created a back-end api that you can see it running here and check out the code here.My background is in Game Development. I have worked in several companies in Vancouver, mainly using C# and C++. I have dipped my toes in Web Development before, but I was never in charge of seeing something through from start to finish. So I decided to create my own web app (at least the back-end part). Since I'm familiar with Python from other projects, I decided to go with the FastAPI framework.So I wanted to do something... doable. But still useful. Something that could have a real application, even if it only lives as a hobby project. An Issue Tracker seemed like a good fit. And with that... Flyswatter was born (well, not yet)! A bug tracker where users could report issues from different projects, comment on them and assign issues to themselves or others. OAuth2 + JWT tokens for authentication🔐pytest running on Github Actions🤖Sentry for error monitoring📊During development I ran into some points of confusion for someone who's getting into a new development environment:The difference between running scripts from docker containers and the docker host. Sometimes it makes no difference, but at other times it does. Something I still need to wrap my head around itThe correct way of managing the database while using Alembic. For example, you should ideally let Alembic generate the tables instead of making them in code. It also creates a table holding the current db revision, which you also shouldn't manually tamper with, unless you know what you're doing. Things like that can create a drift between the database and what Alembic tries to do, risking the migrations to not work at all.Some common features of Python that I'm still not very well versed with, like the different ways to load env vars and for how long they last in memory; unpacking a dictionary to a set of kwargs and also Python's import system.Watching out for which parts of the database aren't covered in Alembic's auto generation. One that I've noticed is that it can add enum types on upgrade but it doesn't remove them on downgrade. nukes all tables except for alembic_versions and other remaining data. It does not go well with alembic inner workings, which may give false positives on migrations or fail them entirely. I lost a few hours trying to find the issue there.In order to rollback database changes (useful for testing), you have to:
Start a db connection that lasts for as long as all the changes you want to rollbackStart a nested subtransactionListen to , which triggers after session.commit()When a transaction ends, start a new subtransactionTo rollback, call the rollback method on the original connection. This will revert all previous nested transactionsAfter about 3 weeks of work, the main features of Flyswatter are complete!You can check out the Github repo and the deployment on Render. I didn't put any work on the front-end, but fortunately FastAPI also makes use of Swagger UI, which generates a docs page with access to all the endpoints.This was a very interesting project which taught me lots in terms of web frameworks and Python in itself. And I'm still not done with it, the next step now is to add background processing with Redis. I'm currently planning on generating a PDF bug report, but I may go with something else in the end.Thank you for reading until this point and feel free to share your thoughts about the project!✌️

item.title: Python Engineering at Microsoft: Simplifying Resource Management in mssql-python through Context Manager
item.content:encoded:
Reviewed by: Sumit Sarabhai and Gaurav SharmaIf you’ve worked with databases in Python, you know the boilerplate: open a connection, create a cursor, run queries, commit or rollback transactions, close cursors and connection. Forgetting just one cleanup step can lead to (open connections) or even . That’s where step in.We’ve introduced context manager support in driver, enabling Python applications to interact with SQL Server and Azure SQL more safely, cleanly, and in a truly Pythonic way. You can install driver using Calling all Python + SQL developers! We invite the community to try out mssql-python and help us shape the future of high-performance .!In Python, the statement is syntactic sugar for resource management. It actively sets up resources when you enter a block and automatically cleans them up when you exit — even if an exception is raised.Think of it as hiring a helper:They before you begin.They when you’re done.If something breaks midway, they for you.Earlier, working with Python applications and SQL server/Azure SQL looked something like this:from mssql_python import connect
conn = connect(connection_string) cursor = conn.cursor()
try: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users") for row in cursor: print(row) finally: cursor.close() conn.close() This works perfectly fine. But imagine if your code had multiple cursors, multiple queries, and exception handling sprinkled all over. Closing every connection and cursor manually becomes tedious and error-prone. Miss a somewhere, and you have a resource leak.That’s where — the context manager — comes to the rescue. not only supports it for connections but also for cursors, which makes resource management nearly effortless.Now comes the real magic — connection-level context managers. When you wrap a connection in a block, several things happen under the hood:If everything succeeds, the transaction is .If an exception occurs, the transaction is .The connection is always when leaving the block.from mssql_python import connect
with connect(connection_string) as conn: cursor = conn.cursor() cursor.execute("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice')") # If no exception → commit happens automatically # If exception → rollback happens automatically # Connection is closed automatically hereEquivalent traditional approach:conn = connect(connection_string) try: cursor = conn.cursor() cursor.execute("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice')") if not conn.autocommit: conn.commit() except: if not conn.autocommit: conn.rollback() raise finally: conn.close()Connection is opened and assigned to conn.All operations inside the block run using this connection. If , transactions are committed. If , uncommitted changes are rolled back. The exception propagates unless handled.Connection is always closed, preventing resource leaks. Perfect for transactional code — inserts, updates, deletes — where you want automatic commit/rollback.Cursors in now support the statement. The context here is tied to the , not the transaction.with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users") for row in cursor: print(row) # Cursor is automatically closed here A new cursor is created. All SQL statements execute using this cursor. The cursor is automatically closed — no need to call manually. The cursor itself doesn’t manage transactions. Commit/rollback is controlled by the connection. If , changes are committed or rolled back at the connection level.If , each statement is committed immediately as it executes.Above code is equivalent to the traditional try-finally approach:cursor = conn.cursor() try: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users") for row in cursor: print(row) finally: cursor.close() Best for read-only queries where you don’t want to worry about cursor leaks.If you just want to ensure the cursor closes properly without worrying about transactions, this is the simplest and safest approach.Image 1: Workflow of Context Manager in Connections and Cursorwith connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 25") for row in cursor: print(row) # Cursor closed, connection still open until block ends # Connection is closed with connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Bob')") cursor.execute("UPDATE users SET age = age + 1 WHERE name = 'Alice'") # Everything committed automatically if no exceptiontry: with connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Charlie')") # Simulate error raise ValueError("Oops, something went wrong") except ValueError as e: print("Transaction rolled back due to:", e) # Connection closed automatically, rollback executedWeb ApplicationsIn a web app where each request inserts or fetches data:def add_user(name): with connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES (?)", (name,))Guarantees commit/rollback automatically.No open connections piling up.Clean, readable, and safe code for high-traffic scenarios.Data Migration / ETLMigrating data between tables:with connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("INSERT INTO archive_users SELECT * FROM users WHERE inactive=1") cursor.execute("DELETE FROM users WHERE inactive=1")If any statement fails, rollback happens automatically.Prevents , keeping data consistent.Automated ReportingRunning multiple queries for analytics:with connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users") user_count = cursor.fetchone()[0] cursor.execute("SELECT department, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY department") for row in cursor: print(row)Cursors closed automatically after each block.Makes scripts modular and maintainable.Financial TransactionsSimple bank transfer example:def transfer_funds(from_account, to_account, amount): with connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - ? WHERE id=?", (amount, from_account)) cursor.execute("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + ? WHERE id=?", (amount, to_account)) on failure ensures money isn’t lost or double-counted.Eliminates verbose error-handling boilerplate.Ad-Hoc Data ExplorationWhen exploring data in scripts or notebooks:with connect(connection_string) as conn: with conn.cursor() as cursor: cursor.execute("SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees") print("Average salary:", cursor.fetchone()[0])Perfect for quick queries.No forgotten calls.Encourages clean, reusable query blocks.Python’s philosophy is “simple is better than complex.” With context managers in , we’ve brought that simplicity to SQL Server interactions with python apps making lives of the developers easier.Next time you’re working with , try wrapping your connections and cursors with . You’ll write less code, make fewer mistakes, and your future self will thank you. Whether it’s a high-traffic web application, an ETL script, or exploratory analysis, context managers simplify life, make code safer, and reduce errors.Remember, context manager will help you with:: No longer try-finally for cursors or connections.Automatic transaction management: Commit or rollback is handled based on success or failure.: Prevents resource leaks with automatic closing.: Nested with blocks clearly show the scope of cursor and connection usage.Check-out the driver and integrate it into your projects.Share your thoughts: Open , suggest features, and contribute to the project.Use Python Driver with Free Azure SQL DatabaseYou can use the Python Driver with the free version of Azure SQL Database! Perfect for testing, development, or learning scenarios without incurring costs.

🚀 A complete journey from a simple Reddit posting tool to a professional-grade CLI with 33+ commands, Docker support, and comprehensive Reddit API integration.As a developer and Reddit enthusiast, I found myself needing to:Post content across multiple subreddits efficientlyMonitor discussions and engage with communitiesResearch topics and find relevant subredditsManage my Reddit presence programmaticallyExisting tools were either too complex, limited in functionality, or required extensive setup. I needed something that was both powerful and easy to use.
The Solution: Reddit CLI Tool I built a comprehensive command-line interface that provides covering every aspect of Reddit interaction. Here's what makes it special:📝 Complete Content Management: Post, edit, delete, and manage Reddit content: Comment, reply, and engage with communities: Search posts, comments, and subreddits across Reddit: View profiles, follow users, and manage relationships: Find trending subreddits and hot posts: Save, unsave, and organize your Reddit content: Send and receive private messages: Upvote and downvote posts and comments: Use subreddit flairs for better categorization: Easy deployment with Docker containers git clone https://github.com/vishwaraja/reddit-cli.git reddit-cli ./setup.sh
./run.sh post askreddit with PRAW (Python Reddit API Wrapper) for easy deployment and consistencyComprehensive error handling with exponential backoff to comply with Reddit's API limitsBeautiful terminal output with emojis and clear formatting

🔍 Discovery & Search (6 commands)./run.sh search-subreddits 10 ./run.sh search-posts ./run.sh hot programming 10 ./run.sh trending 10

�� ./run.sh responses 20 ./run.sh monitor 60 The tool features a comprehensive help system with emoji-rich commands and detailed examples:Shows all 33+ commands with clear descriptions and usage examples.: Schedule and manage content across multiple subreddits: Monitor responses and engage with your audience: Find trending topics and popular discussions: Share your open-source projects with relevant communities: Participate in programming and tech subreddits: Find and share educational content: Gather insights from Reddit discussions: Monitor trending topics and community sentiment: Study online communities and social behavior: Monitor mentions and engage with customers: Understand customer needs and preferences: Build and nurture online communities
Technical Challenges & Solutions Reddit has strict API rate limits. I implemented: for failed requests with configurable delaysUser-friendly error messages explaining rate limitsComprehensive error handling for: (401 errors) (403 errors) and connection issuesMade the tool Docker-ready for: without Python environment conflicts across different systems environment
Code Quality & Best Practices throughout the codebase for all functions with clear separation of concernsConsistent error handling patterns with emojis and clear formatting for maximum compatibility with examples and documentation for easy deployment with proper structure covering all major Reddit APIs error handling and rate limiting for easy deploymentComprehensive documentation and help system with MIT licenseBeautiful terminal output with emojis and clear formattingIntuitive command structure that's easy to rememberComprehensive help system with examples with proper rate limitingWhile the tool is already feature-complete, potential future enhancements include: functionality features for non-technical usersDocker (recommended) or Python 3.11+ git clone https://github.com/vishwaraja/reddit-cli.git reddit-cli
./setup.sh
reddit_config.json.example reddit_config.json
./run.sh Click "Create App" or "Create Another App"Fill in the form:
: Any name (e.g., "My Reddit CLI"): Select "script": Note down your and Building this Reddit CLI tool was an incredible journey that taught me:: How to work with complex APIs like Reddit's: The importance of robust error handling and user feedback: How small details like emojis and clear formatting make a big difference: The power of containerization for easy deployment: The value of building tools that others can use and contribute toThe tool is now with 33+ commands, comprehensive error handling, and professional-grade features. It's open source, well-documented, and ready for the community to use and contribute to.Start simple, iterate fast: We began with basic posting and gradually added features: Beautiful output and clear help make tools more usableError handling is crucial: Robust error handling prevents user frustration: Good documentation makes tools accessible to everyoneOpen source benefits everyone: Sharing tools helps the entire communityWhat do you think?
